reasoning: The resolution criteria require the absolute nominal value of Q4 2025 Federal Government Current Expenditures (FGEXPND) to be at least $250 billion lower than Q4 2024. Historically, nominal federal spending almost never shrinks year-over-year outside of post-war or post-pandemic demobilizations, because built-in inflation, mandatory spending increases (Social Security/Medicare), and interest on the debt naturally push spending up by hundreds of billions annually. To achieve a $250B absolute reduction, Congress would have to implement cuts of $400B-$600B just to offset this baseline growth. Because Trump has pledged not to cut Social Security or Medicare, and defense spending is typically protected by Republicans, these massive cuts would have to come from a very small slice of discretionary spending, which is politically impossible even with Republican control. Thus, the probability of an absolute >$250B drop in nominal spending in one year is extremely close to zero.
